coredata.dc:title: A coupled multi-physics study on Li-ion batteries under impact loading

Huang, Jiaqi
Lu, Guoxing
Steven, Grant P.
coredata.dc:description: As lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) become more prevalent in electric vehicles (EVs), understanding their mechanical, electrical, and thermal behaviour under impact loading is crucial for crash safety. This study proposes a cost-effective numerical framework that integrates a coupled thermo-electro-chemo-mechanical model to predict the temperature distribution and mechanical failure of LIB cells under various impact conditions. The geometric data of the battery is obtained using computed tomography (CT) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) prior to impact tests, followed by mechanical characterisation of each jellyroll layer. Four distinct impact scenarios—bending with and without electrolyte leakage, and compression causing full or partial layer delamination—are established to calibrate and validate the model for two different battery types. These scenarios are designed to encompass potential phenomena, such as shell casing damage and electrolyte leakage, which may occur during a real crash. A novel distributed Randle circuit is introduced to simulate electrolyte leakage and casing shell failure, incorporating virtual battery and virtual resistance components. The numerical model is validated through in-house experimental tests. The framework accurately predicts the temperature field, voltage drop, and mechanical delamination of LIB cells, demonstrating that the voltage drop correlates not only with separator failure but also with electrolyte leakage.

The figure illustrates the sequence of events during the drop tests from the 1 m and 3 m heights, highlighting the contact phase (row (a)), the progression of impact and electrolyte leakage (row (b)), and the peak jet flow of electrolyte for H = 3 m case (row (c)). Notable differences in electrolyte behaviour and jelly roll ejection are observed, particularly in the higher drop height cases. Electrolyte leakage can be observed in H = 3 m case in comparison with H = 1 m case scenarios (row (d)). As the impactor continues to descend, the pressure of the electrolyte forces the jelly roll to be ejected (row (e)).

Notably, the cell surface temperature remains unchanged after the impact test, as the initial temperature of the battery matched the ambient temperature. This stability in temperature, in conjunction with the observed voltage drop, suggests no significant thermal changes occurred; (e) simulated temperature field after the impact test.

head.ce:title: A coupled multi-physics study on Li-ion batteries under impact loading

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) become more prevalent in electric vehicles (EVs), understanding their mechanical, electrical, and thermal behaviour under impact loading is crucial for crash safety. This study proposes a cost-effective numerical framework that integrates a coupled thermo-electro-chemo-mechanical model to predict the temperature distribution and mechanical failure of LIB cells under various impact conditions. The geometric data of the battery is obtained using computed tomography (CT) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) prior to impact tests, followed by mechanical characterisation of each jellyroll layer. Four distinct impact scenarios—bending with and without electrolyte leakage, and compression causing full or partial layer delamination—are established to calibrate and validate the model for two different battery types. These scenarios are designed to encompass potential phenomena, such as shell casing damage and electrolyte leakage, which may occur during a real crash. A novel distributed Randle circuit is introduced to simulate electrolyte leakage and casing shell failure, incorporating virtual battery and virtual resistance components. The numerical model is validated through in-house experimental tests. The framework accurately predicts the temperature field, voltage drop, and mechanical delamination of LIB cells, demonstrating that the voltage drop correlates not only with separator failure but also with electrolyte leakage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries are becoming a key energy source for electric vehicles (EV). However, EV safety related to impact of such batteries during accident remains a major concern to date [1,2]. The high risk of serious hazards such as fires and release of harmful or explosive materials [3] underscores the necessity for a more thorough comprehension of the events and conditions leading to battery failure [4]. Since 2014, there have been numerous high-profile fire incidents involving EVs, many of which were attributed to mechanical damage and thermal runaway induced by collision [5]. The safety issues of these high energy density storage devices and increasing demand for a robust numerical model have drawn rising attention of numerous researchers and developers from a multidisciplinary perspective. The growing number of documented incidents involving lithium-ion battery firing in EVs [6] highlights the significance of these safety concerns [7] and emphasises the importance for further investigating critical factors related internal short circuits (ISC) [8–10], thermal runaway [11–15] alongside mechanical failures [16].

Over the past few years, extensive research interests have been directed to modelling of Lithium-ion batteries, encompassing some forms of electrochemical models in static state of cell, mechanical models ranging from quasi-static to dynamic loading [17–22], battery thermal management system [23], and multiphysics models [23,24] across different length scales ranging from nano to cell levels [25–29]. To a certain extent, each of such existing literature models helps gain some understanding on impact-led battery failure under specifically prescribed loading conditions. Recent studies [30,31], have investigated lithium-ion battery failures under extremely high transient impacts, proposing mechanical impact dynamic (MID) models and highlighting how separator thickness and material properties influence battery resilience during high-g events.

The early battery models had focused on the electrochemical aspect, representing service conditions without external impact loads. Compared to extensive electrochemical works, coupled multiphysics studies on mechanical and thermal responses to impact of a battery necessitate further research [6–17,19,25,27,32–35], where their characteristic properties are interconnected in a fairly sophisticated manner [36]. Additionally, recent data-driven approaches such as the use of E-LSTM and CNN–LSTM models for state-of-health (SOH) prediction and anomaly detection in lithium-ion batteries have been developed to enhance monitoring and reliability [37,38]. It was unveiled that severe mechanical deformation of battery can be a trigger for internal short-circuits [39,40]. In turn, drastic layer deformation in Li-ion battery to a considerable extent affects the electrochemical performance [4] of active materials in electrode coatings, ultimately altering the electrochemical and electrical properties. For this reason, a comprehensive consideration of various multiphysics features and their intricate coupling effects is necessary to accurately predict the behaviour of these cells.

As an effective tool, finite element (FE) modelling has become a cost-effective approach for predicting the battery behaviour over the past decade. In literature, there have been three commonly used modelling approaches, namely detailed layered models [41–44], homogenised modelling [17,24,45], and representative volume element (RVE) model [46,47]. To handle sophisticated multi-material system in battery, homogenised modelling [48] utilises some forms of averaged material properties for simulations, which reduces the need of extensive testing for model characterisation compared to RVE modelling [46], and offers high computational efficiency compared to the detailed modelling [17]. Due to considerable simplicity in such a homogenised model, nevertheless, it sometimes over or under estimates the impact responses under different loading conditions [49]. More importantly, homogenised modelling is difficult (if not impossible) to simulate delamination of the layers, the failure mechanism [41,47], locus of separator failure [41], and thermal runaway in details [19].

Unlike the homogenised approach, RVE only models the details of a key yet small part of battery for specific analysis [51]. For example, Kermani et al. [53] investigated the buckling behaviour of lithium-ion cells using a two-step approach, integrating analytical modelling and Representative Volume Element (RVE) simulations. The study demonstrated that the RVE model could predict the anisotropic in-plane material response and buckling stresses by incorporating detailed interactions between the battery layers while simplifying experimental requirements. However, the RVE model relies heavily on accurate geometry and material property measurements, which can introduce errors if these parameters are imprecise. Additionally, its periodicity assumptions and calibration requirements limit its adaptability to irregular cell geometries or non-standard configurations. While the model showed good agreement with analytical results for pouch cells, discrepancies observed in the elliptical cell predictions highlight potential limitations in generalizing the approach across different battery types.

The detailed model, on the other hand, models each individual layer individually, enabling to capture specific mechanical behaviour, such as layer delamination in the course of crushing. Under certain loading conditions, such as axial compression, only a proper detailed-layer model could work to unveil multiphysics realistically [44]. This is primarily due to the unique assembly of anode and cathode layers, resulting in an asymmetry between the top and bottom parts of cylindrical cells, where the geometric configurations vary [53]. One of the main challenges in modelling the battery details is how to consider the effect of electrolyte in the battery that might reduce during dynamic test as a result of rupture in the shell casing. In a study by Gilaki et al. [50], dynamic compression tests were performed on stacks of dry separators, a constituent component of batteries, as well as wet separators immersed in electrolyte, to evaluate the mechanical responses. Their research uncovered a insignificant difference between the dry and wet separators, with the wet components exhibiting increased strength at a higher loading rate. The research also showed that heterogenous modelling and homogenised modelling of the impact loading can lead to different results in modelling the impact loading versus time [50], thereby affecting battery failure and thermal response. Note that one of the main shortcomings of the detailed model is that it can be rather time-consuming if all of the physical phenomena such as thermal, mechanical, electrochemical processes of the incident are considered together exhaustively. In comparison, Lian et al.'s study highlights that while the homogenised model is less computationally demanding, it tends to overpredict the force level during in-plane compression of Li-ion batteries compared to the detailed layer model [48]. Further, the detailed model might enable to predict deformation and failure mechanisms accurately under quasi-static loading, but may not work properly in dynamic loading scenarios [54] due to the activation effect of electrolyte. Note that while quasi-static loading scenarios of detailed cellular layer models can offer valuable insights, real-world accidents are often dominated by dynamic loadings [19,23,32,54–58].

In this present research, we attempt to develop a hybrid modelling approach by incorporating the homogenised model and detailed layer model. Such a hybrid modelling strategy aims to address the limitations of each method while harnessing their individual benefits. Further, we modify the Randle parameters to calibrate and predict the temperature following impact analysis, discerning the presence or absence of an electrolyte leakage.

This paper is structured as follows. After this introduction, Section 2 describes the proposed experimental materials and methods, followed by numerical methods in Section 3. Then Section 4 presents the results and discussions. Finally, Section 5 presents concluding remarks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental materials and methods
Geometric characterisation of the cell components
ce:section.ce:para: An accurate numerical model requires a thorough measurement of the geometric details of battery structures beforehand. Hence, we utilised X-ray microscopic computed tomography (μCT) technique to scan internal microstructures of each layer of the battery precisely prior to mechanical tests. Specifically, the SkyScan2214, operating at 120 kV and 80 microamps, was adopted for allowing capturing high-resolution images with a pixel size around 7 μm.

Additionally, a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) Zeiss EVO 50 was used to measure the thicknesses of the separator, anode, and cathode layers for detailed numerical modelling. For preparing the SEM samples, the jellyroll was taken from the shell casing, then the sample was cut properly and placed on a holder carefully with vacuuming for 20 min to extract the liquid electrolyte. Notably, no resin was added to harden the layers [22] to prevent possible increase in layer separation. As illustrated in Fig. 1(c), the thickness of one layer varies from 175 to 210 μm across its radial direction.

It should be noted that the thickness of the shell casing was measured at various locations across the axial length of the battery using the μCT scanner. As depicted in Fig. 2 , the thickness of the shell casing is not uniform, with the minimum thickness occurring in the middle of the battery. Furthermore, the μCT scanner also enables accurate measurement of the location of each layer relative to the centre of the battery and the diameter of the hollow cylinder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material characterisation of the individual layer of the Li-ion battery
ce:section.ce:para: The cathode is constructed from a slender aluminium substrate, encompassing active materials, conductive additive, and binder components. The term “component” here refers to a mixture of active materials, conductive additive, and a binder, in which a minor portion of conductive additive is integrated into the lithium oxide-based active materials, alongside a binder for cohesion. These compounds are in a form of finely powdered micro-particles. When coated onto both sides of an aluminium substrate, they collectively form the cathode materials. Here, the cathode active materials consist of lithium combined with various metals, each imparting distinct characteristics depending on their type and proportion. For instance, nickel (Ni) contributes to high capacity, manganese (Mn) and cobalt (Co) enhance safety, and aluminium (Al) boosts the power output of the battery. Anode active material is composed of graphite, and the current collector is made from copper [25]. These constituents characterise the material properties for the modelling study.

In this research, the material properties of individual battery components are characterized. These components are sourced from disassembled lithium-ion batteries and shaped accordingly for material testing purposes. Specifically, the jellyroll component layers were cut into a rectangular shape measuring 20 mm in width and 50 mm in length.

An Instron 5500 testing system equipped with a 10 kN load cell was employed to perform the tensile tests on the battery components with varying loading rates ranging from 1 mm/min to 20 mm/min. The mechanical responses for each layer are obtained in Fig. 3 . To characterise the antistrophic properties of battery materials, the tensile tests for battery components were conducted in the two orthogonal directions: namely machine direction (MD) and transverse direction (TD), as illustrated in Fig. 1(e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental setup and dynamic test procedure
ce:section.ce:para: The dynamic tests aim to obtain fundamental data on the overall response of the battery cell, thereby identifying the initiation of short circuits resulting from impact loading, and measuring the temperature rise as a result of leakage induced by battery failure. Not only were the measurements taken for the load-displacement relationship of the whole battery, but also on its voltage output, along with monitoring any temperature rise associated with a short circuit. Given that the cylindrical cells are typically rated at 2600–5000 mAh at full charge [19,59], the batteries were discharged to less than 15 % as a precautionary measure to reduce the risk of thermal runaway in the event of a short circuit [60].

The 18,650 and 26,650 cells are the commercial lithium-ion batteries widely available in practice; and they also offer certain testing advantages due to their commonality and applicability, especially 18,650 being employed in Tesla EVs [42]. Their compact size permits use of standard mechanical testing facility with minimal cleanup required in case of electrolyte leakage, even when endcaps of testing cells are removed. Detailed specifications of these two batteries used for tests in this study are provided in Table 1 .

The Phantom v2512 high-speed camera (Wayne, USA) was utilised to monitor impacting process, which enables to take over 25,000 frames-per-second. It was connected to the MTS computer via ultra-fast 10GB Ethernet for data transfer. The camera's ISO sensitivity ranges from Mono 32,000D to 100,000 T, ensuring to take high quality images even under low-light conditions. A versatile Micro-Epsilon's optoNCDT 1607 sensor (Ortenburg, Germany) was employed for displacement measurements. The 5015 A universal laboratory charge amplifier (Winterthur, Switzerland), featuring a 1-channel configuration, serves as an important tool for measuring impact force through the piezoelectric sensors. The 3 Series Mixed Domain Tektronix Oscilloscope (Oregon, USA), equipped with 4 analogue channels, 16 digital channels, and 1 spectrum analyser, was used to analyse the signals in our experiments. For further clarification, readers may refer to Fig. 4 .

To prepare for the tests, the cells underwent at least one full charge/discharge cycle at a constant 1 amp current to verify their nominal capacity. All the tests were conducted with the cells fully discharged, reaching less than 85 % depth of discharge (state of charge, SOC, of less than 15 %). Kisters et al. [54] demonstrated that the mechanical behaviour of batteries remains unaffected by the state of charge prior to a short circuit. Therefore, it is presumed that the discharged state does not compromise the validity of the mechanical tests while mitigating the risks of post-test thermal runaway.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drop-weight impact tests
ce:section.ce:para: Four case scenarios were considered for the drop-weight impact tests, two for three-point bending impact and another two for the flat compression impact. The first customised drop-weight test was set for three-point bending impact, aimed to mimic crash scenarios, in which the force is concentrated on the middle region of the battery. In this loading case, the electrolyte leakage might be zero or nonzero depending on the momentum exerted on the battery, while some separator layers might fail in the MD direction. The impactor and the three-point bending setup are illustrated in Fig. 5 (a), in which the radius of the two supporting rollers is 10 mm, and the span between their centres is 50 mm. It is worth mentioning that the wires depicted in Fig. 5(b) are connected to the battery terminals through a spot-welding technique. The maximum impact energy of the system with the customised impactor shown in Fig. 5(a) was 6500 J, adjustable by tuning the dropping mass and/or height. Considering the energy absorption capacity of the battery specimens, the dropping mass and maximum height of the impactor were set to be 5.97 kg and 3.0 m after some pilot tests, respectively. While the theoretical impact velocity was calculated to be 7.67 m/s, the measured impact velocity was 7.4 m/s, likely due to frictional loss between the battery and impactor as well as the guide rails. It is important to note that the three-point bending impact test was conducted at least twice for each defined case, including different drop heights and the presence of electrolyte leakage, to ensure accuracy and replicability.

In the second case scenario, the flat impactor moved downward, with a velocity varying by gravity. A dedicated steel impactor, as depicted in Fig. 4, was utilised, featuring the dimensions substantially larger than those of the battery specimen, which possibly leads to maximum electrolyte leakage anticipated. Furthermore, this setup is designed to induce maximum delamination of most layers, a phenomenon may not be replicated by a homogenised or RVE model, accurately. In this configuration, both the supporting platen and the impactor are flat, allowing for generating the maximum electrolyte leakage of the battery cell during a real crash incidence of EVs.

In the abovementioned both impact cases, the temperature measurements were conducted using a Protech hand-held thermal IR camera before and after the tests. The drop tower test chamber featured a safety window at the front for video recording purposes as illustrated in Fig. 4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Numerical modelling
ce:section.ce:para: There are some challenges for predicting temperature field in Li-ion battery in literature [23,61]. The main issue is that the computational cost for a fully-coupled mechanical-thermal-electrochemical model can be fairly high [57]. For this reason, some of the authors ignore the coupled effects of electrochemical and thermomechanical fields, and solely predict the mechanical responses [39,62]. Nevertheless, most of the existing models suggested for thermo-electro-chemo-mechanical coupling are used to predict the perpendicular punch force onto pouch battery in a quasi-static condition [63].

Eq. (1) reflects Newton's second law applied to a deformable body under dynamic (time-dependent) conditions, which is crucial for simulating impact events on battery materials.

The constitutive relation linking stress and strain for the battery materials, which often undergo large deformations and heating during impact, is expressed as:

•

is the plastic strain tensor (permanent deformation due to yielding).
This constitutive model accounts for the thermo-mechanical coupling in battery materials: thermal strains arise from heating during impact (e.g., due to internal friction and chemical reactions),
while plastic strains represent the irreversible deformation caused by exceeding the material's yield strength. The indices i , j , k and l equal 1, 2, or 3 corresponding to x , y and z directions in three-dimensional space.

Eq. (3) represents the linearized strain-displacement relationship under the assumption of small deformations and small rotations.
This assumption is typically valid for certain scenarios where the deformation remains relatively small.
However, under severe impact conditions — as often encountered during battery crashes — the deformation can become large, and the small strain assumption may no longer hold.

In such cases, the Green-Lagrange strain tensor should be used to accurately capture both the linear and nonlinear deformation effects. It is expressed as:

In cases involving large deformation (which can happen during severe impacts on batteries), the full finite strain tensor would need to be considered.

(diagonal components) and 0 otherwise (off-diagonal components). Thus, thermal strains only affect the normal components of the strain tensor, without inducing shear strains.

In the context of battery impact simulations, the mechanical failure of the shell casing material (characterized by the equivalent stress exceeding the yield limit) typically occurs well before other forms of internal failure, such as internal short-circuiting between the anode and aluminium components [64].

For the battery separator material, a maximum strain failure criterion was applied, expressed as:

To obtain the calibrated temperature of quiescent air, it is necessary to consider that the battery temperature was recorded several minutes after the impact test, ensuring that there is no risk of thermal runaway and allowing for the removal of impactor from the battery. Following this, we placed the battery on another surface to measure the surface temperature with the thermal camera. After the battery being impacted and measuring the temperature, we assumed that the surface temperature of the battery remained unchanged at the instant. Therefore, we use the highest temperature of the battery in each experiment for assigning the thermal conductivity of quiescent air. For instance, if the highest temperature measured by the thermal camera is 363 K, the corresponding thermal conductivity will be around 0.3 W / m ∙ K [65].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical model
ce:section.ce:para: Equivalent circuit models are broadly used in electrochemical modelling [24]. The most recent modelling technique for the electro-chemical process in battery is the first order distributed Randle circuit model [69,70], which comprises a capacitor, C10, in parallel with a resistor, R10, and is equivalent to these capacitor and resistor in series with a resistor, R0, as illustrated in Fig. 8(a). One approach for calibrating R0, R10, and C10 is to carry out the hybrid pulse power characterisation (HPPC) test, which might take around 20 h [24]. For calibrating the Randle parameters, we propose here a homogenised model which includes the distributed Randle circuit model for modelling the quasi-static loading of the punch test on the pouch battery. The battery was charged to 100 % and followed by a 10-s discharge and charge pulse at different discrete State of Charge (SOC) ranging from 90 to 10 % [24].

It is important to clarify that the basic first-order distributed Randle circuit with calibrated parameters from the HPPC tests may not accurately predict [71] temperature variations in such case scenarios as battery leakage, or the instances in which the jellyroll is subjected to impact loads. To overcome the abovementioned issue, we propose to modify the Randle model, by adding two additional components, as illustrated in Fig. 8 (b). These two new parameters, named
U v
and
R f
, can be determined by the experiment, given as,
(17)
R f
= H
F _ S
× RSOC × P ×
− R 0
(18)
U v
= H
F _ H
× U × RSOC
in which
U v
has three components, namely H represents the Heaviside function, U denotes the normal Randle voltage, and RSOC is a parameter to be calibrated through the simulation. F _ S and F _ L are logic numbers, if there is a failure at the shell casing, F _ L is equal to 1, otherwise, it is 0. Similarly, F _ S will be 1 if there is a failure at the separator, otherwise it will be 0.

The rationale for including
U v
stems from the fact that during propagation of a crack through the shell casing, there is an egress of electrolyte from the battery medium. Consequently, the calibrated parameters derived from the HPPC tests may no longer hold validity. The incorporation of
R f
corresponds to the instances of localized separator failure. In the event of separator failure, the cathode coating and anode coating come into direct contact. In such scenarios,
R f
serves to mitigate this effect by reducing the equivalent resistance, denoted as R 0 . The parameter P in
R f
requires calibration based on the results of the three-point bending simulation.

Electric flux is obtained by the Ohm equation:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Numerical modelling to predict the short circuit and temperature field
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, LS-Dyna version R14.0 (ANSYS, Canonsburg, US) was utilised to model the cylindrical battery cell for multi-physics simulation of the three-point bending and compression dynamic tests, respectively.

The generic numerical modelling procedures for these two tests involved two distinct finite element analyses:
•
First FE analysis: we first modelled the 18,650 batteries in a mechanical environment to determine the force applied to the battery. This initial analysis was completed in few minutes, providing a significant time advantage compared to the 24-h duration required for a full multi-physics simulation.
•
Second FE analysis: subsequently, the calibrated parameters obtained from the initial analysis were used in a coupled multi-physical finite element analysis. This model included 2280 solid elements for modelling the supporters, impactor and the two tabs on both sides of the battery, 440 shell elements for the shell casing, and 800 composite elements representing the internal layers of the battery, as illustrated in Fig. 7(c). The shell elements were defined with dimensions of 4.1 mm × 3.23 mm and a thickness of 0.3 mm here.

Each of the layers in the Tshell composite element was modelled using a crushable foam model with a density ( ρ ) of 1600 kg / m 3 and a Young's modulus ( E ) of 500 MPa. The plastic behaviour of the layers was modelled using Eq. (22), as discussed by Sahraei et al. [59] for modelling battery leakage,
(22)
σ = dβε 2
in which the parameter β was set to be 550 for static loading conditions in Sahraei's model [59]. The authors introduce the parameter d to account for the dynamic behaviour of the system, d needs to be calibrated for static and dynamic simulation. For the static loading d will be set to 1like the model suggested by Sahraei et al. [55].

Bulla et al. [19] suggested that the plastic behaviour of the jelly roll under dynamic loading increases exponentially during thermal runaway. Therefore, we applied Bulla et al.'s material model for the jelly roll when the battery experiences no leakage, and the temperature is significantly higher than that in the leakage scenario. The thickness of each layer of the jellyroll was varied between 0.2 mm and 0.45 mm for geometric modelling.

The interaction between the supports and the shell casing, as well as between the impactor and the shell casing, was modelled in LS-DYNA using the AUTOMATIC_SURFACE_TO_SURFACE algorithm. Additionally, the contact between the jelly roll and the shell casing was defined using the CONTACT_TIED_SURFACE_TO_SURFACE algorithm.

The shell casing was modelled using the piecewise linear plasticity material from the LS-DYNA library. For the steel shell casing, the following parameters were used: Young's modulus E = 200
GPa , Poisson's ratio ν = 0.3 , and yield strength
σ yield
= 450 MPa [59]. The friction coefficient between the rigid bodies and the battery was set to be 0.3, which was obtained after conducting a pilot FE analysis for correlation. Regarding the boundary conditions, the nodes of the shell casing were attached to the point of contact, as shown in Fig. 6 , and the line of contact, as depicted in Fig. 7 . The initial velocities corresponding to the specified heights of 1 m, 2 m, and 3 m were determined using the drop test velocity from Table 2 .

The thermal and electrochemical boundary conditions described in Eqs. (12) to (15), (19), and (20) are illustrated in Figs. 6 and 7, respectively. The heat capacity for the entire battery was set to be 1420 J/kg/K [65]. To capture the electromagnetic effect, two tabs were modelled at either end of the battery, each with a thickness of 0.21 mm. Iso-potential values of 0 V and 0.05 V were assigned to each tab. The electrical conductivity values for the anode and cathode were set to 5.88 ×
10 7
and 3.7 ×
10 7
siemens per metre ( S / m ), respectively [72]. To integrate the electrochemical model into the finite element (FE) simulation, we utilised a modified distributed Randle circuit between each node within the battery, as proposed in Fig. 8(b). The parameters for the Randles model will be calibrated based on the coupled multi-physics FEA simulation and are configured as follows: the active electrolyte resistance (R0) is set to 0.02 Ω in both discharge and charge directions, the impedance (R10) is set to 0.008 Ω, and the capacitance (C10) is 100 units. The charge transfer resistance (CQ) is set to 0.03, with the initial state of charge at 10 %. After calibration with the temperature field from the experiment, the calibrated charge (Q) is set to 1. The temperature used for the Randles circuit parameters is derived from the thermal solver in the coupled multiphysics finite element analysis.

It should be noted that for modelling the 26,650 batteries, we just used one single multifield FE analysis. Since we measure the temperature 20 mins after finishing the test, we need to conduct another thermal FE analysis for correlating the final temperature field. In the case of compression test, the temperature rise was negligible due to the complete rupture of the shell casing. Therefore, only the mechanical simulation of the battery was modelled, which took about three hours using a 13th Gen Intel® Core™ i9-13900KF processor running at 3.00 GHz with 128 GB of RAM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
ce:section.ce:para: In this section we use the four experimental case scenarios to validate the FE models, two with the shell casing ruptures, one without, and one for the jelly roll.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Three-point bending impact test without external leakage of electrolyte: case study on 26650 at drop test of height = 2 m
ce:section.ce:para: In the first experimental case scenario, an impactor was dropped onto a 26,650 cell from a height of 2 m. At this height, no electrolyte leakage from the cell's casing was observed. The two replicative experiments were conducted, in which testing samples 1 and 2 refer to the repeated tests conducted under the same conditions.

The maximum force recorded for sample 1 was 16.80 kN at a displacement of 10.51 mm, occurring 1.79 ms after impact; and a similar force level of 15.11 kN was recorded for sample 2, at a displacement of 11.4 mm, 1.84 ms after impact. The corresponding voltages for samples 1 and 2 were 3.1 V and 3.2 V, respectively. As shown in Fig. 9 , the voltage decreases gradually after impact. The simulated maximum first peak force was 16.1 kN, which falls between the maximum first peak forces recorded in the two repeated experiments. This further validates the results of the coupled multiphysical simulation. It is worth mentioning that in the force-time curve for the first sample test, a flat line appears instead of the second peak. This occurred because maximum force recording in the oscilloscope was initially set to be 18.02 kN, meaning that any force exceeding this value was not accurately captured though it is not far from that plotted from the second test as seen in Fig. 9. As noted by other battery researchers, the first force peak, which is associated with battery failure and leads to a voltage drop, is more crucial as it can directly trigger thermal runaway [47].

The temperature field was measured before the test, as shown in Fig. 10(a), and again, 20 min after the test, as depicted in Figs. 10 (b)-(d). The primary cause of temperature elevation is typically due to a short circuit [28], which can result from separator failure and manifest as a voltage drop, as illustrated in Fig. 9. It is important to note that no rupture of the shell casing was observed in both the experiment and simulation

After 20 min of the impact test, the temperature started increasing, reaching a maximum peak temperature of 95.4 °C at some points on the shell casing surface, as illustrated in Fig. 10 (b). Following this, the average temperature began to gradually increase, reaching 90.4 °C, as seen in Fig. 10 (c), and then started to decrease to 89.4 °C, as shown in Fig. 10 (d).

To simulate the temperature distribution more precisely, we conducted a transient thermal FE analysis over the 20-min duration, with the results shown in Figs. 10 (e)-(h). It should be noted that the heat convection coefficient in Eq. (13) varies because the battery was moved from an enclosed chamber to a table. For simplicity, instead of accounting for convective heat transfer, we assumed that the temperature on both sides of the battery, which are in contact with the jelly rolls, remained constant at the near table temperature of 15 °C. This approach enables to mimics the effects of forced and free convection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Three-point bending impact tests with external electrolyte leakage (at different heights and initial voltages for 18,650 lithium-ion cells)
ce:section.ce:para: In the subsequent phase of the experiment, the 18,650 lithium-ion cells underwent three-point bending tests at varying heights and different initial voltages. Note that the state of charge (SOC) for all the batteries was less than 10 %. The initial voltage readings were 3.28 V for the battery dropped from a height of 3 m, 3.081 V for the battery at 1 m, and 2.11 V for the jelly roll dropped from 1 m.

In Fig. 11 , “H” represents the height, and the columns labelled as “I,” “II,” and “III” correspond to the following testing conditions:
•
I: dropped from Height of 1 m (H = 1 m) for the 18,650 whole battery cell.
•
II: dropped from Height of 1 m (H = 3 m) for the 18,650 whole battery cell.
•
III: dropped from Height of 1 m (H = 1 m) for the 18,650 jellyroll.

This setup differentiates the height, and the type of components tested, either the entire battery cell or the jellyroll component. In the following paragraphs, it should be noted that when referencing Fig. 11 (e-III), it indicates the row “e” and column “III”. This notation helps clarify the specific tests within the figure being discussed.

In Fig. 11 (a-I), (a-II) and (a-III), The impactor makes its initial contact with the batteries in these three different scenarios. In Fig. 11 (b-II), for the case of H = 3 m, the higher velocity causes the impactor to penetrate further, resulting in rupture of the shell casing and the leakage of electrolyte. As seen in Fig. 11 (c-II), the electrolyte jet flow reaches its peak in the H = 3 m scenario, whereas no electrolyte leakage is observed in Fig. 11 (c-I) and Fig. 11 (c-III). As the impactor continues to descend, the pressure of the electrolyte forces the jelly roll on the left side of the split battery to be ejected, as seen in Fig. 11 (e-II). To clearly observe the jet flow, readers may refer to the video attached to this paper.

Notably, despite a significant electrolyte jet flow under a 3 m drop height, no such a flow is observed at 1 m drop height. This suggests that in the H = 1 m case, some electrolyte may remain within the shell casing, potentially leading to a slightly higher temperature post-impact compared to the H = 3 m scenario. When examining the jelly roll, some electrolyte might have lost during extraction from the shell casing. Additionally, the impactor may cause localized failures within the jelly roll layers, leading to elevated temperature in the region.

At Fig. 11 (e-III), electrolyte leakage is observed from the left split side of the battery, a phenomenon not seen in Fig. 11 (e-I). This discrepancy may be due to the end caps of the battery, which prevent the escape of electrolyte through the positive and negative terminals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 18650 Li-ion cell at drop test of height = 1 m
ce:section.ce:para: When a rupture at the shell casing occurs, it was found that the H = 1 m scenario leads to the highest temperature. For this reason, we conducted the three-point bending impact tests on the four samples further, each with different initial voltages, 3.08, 2.97, 3.21, and 3.55 V, as shown in Table 3 . The first peak forces, maximum forces, and the occurrence of short circuits were all recorded in Table 3. It should be noted that the data for Sample 1 was recorded only up to 3 milliseconds due to human error during the oscilloscope setup, where the data acquisition parameters were not properly configured for longer recording. As a result, the available data history after 3 ms is limited.

The force history exerted on the supports of three-point bending tests is illustrated in Fig. 12 . Due to the movement of the battery and the cylindrical supports, the force vs. time curves of the four samples present more fluctuations, indicating frequent variations in force during the testing process. The calibrated parameters, 4 and 0.25, for β in Eq. (21) were not directly responsible for determining the second peak forces but were instrumental in capturing key characteristics of the behaviour leading to these peaks. The experimental second peak forces, 9.61 kN, 9.91 kN, and 10.82 kN, fell between the simulation peak forces associated with these β values, which were 10.93 kN and 9.46 kN, respectively. This indicates a good correlation between the simulations and the experimental results.”

As seen in Fig. 12, while the sudden voltage drop in Sample 4 starts at 4.14 ms, leading to a short circuit of the battery, no such voltage drop is observed in Samples 2 and 3. In the simulation, we can conclude that a β value of 0.3025 might lead to a short circuit, while a β value less than 0.3025 might not result in a short circuit. In an ideal situation for this case scenario, we would expect to see similar level of the first and second peak values. However, in dynamic conditions such as the drop test, there exist multiple instabilities during experimental tests.

The impactor and supporters cannot be perfectly guided due to acceleration of the impactor, which results in multiple peaks. Typically speaking, the initial voltage does not significantly affect the mechanical responses when there is electrolyte leakage. Note that the primary effect of leakage is on the internal chemistry and safety of the battery rather than on the mechanical stress experienced by the battery components.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 18650 Li-ion cell at drop test of height = 3 m
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 13 plots the force against time, alongside the corresponding voltage data, with an initial voltage of 3.24 V. Notably, the voltage begins to decrease gradually before 1.54 ms, at which point it suddenly drops just before the second major peak in the force curve. Unlike the sharp voltage drop observed in Fig. 12, the decline in voltage here is more gradual leading up to the 1.54 ms mark.

The temperature after the 3 m impact is significantly lower than that in the 1 m scenario. There are several reasons for this. (1) The electrolyte leakage, as shown in Fig. 11 (d-II), is much more severe than that in the 1 m case. Even if separator failure occurs, the reduced amount of electrolyte may not be sufficient to cause a significant temperature increase after the impact. (2) After the battery splits into two parts, the rupture of the shell casing is so extensive that the positive and negative terminals cannot transfer electrons effectively. (3) The jelly roll emerges from the left part of the broken shell casing, increasing the connection between the positive and negative ends. This altered configuration may produce a temperature rise. (4) As shown in Fig. 13 (c), the penetration of air into the jelly roll could vaporize some of the electrolyte, reducing its quantity. With less electrolyte, there is less potential for chemical reactions in the event of separator failure, thereby resulting in a smaller temperature increase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 18650 Jellyroll Li-ion at drop test of height = 1 m
ce:section.ce:para: The initial voltage was measured to be approximately 3.1 V before removing the shell casing. Upon extracting the steel shell, some electrolyte is released or vaporised, resulting in a lower starting voltage for the jellyroll of 2.09 V. This indicates that more than one-third of the voltage drop can be attributed to the absence of an enclosure, such as the shell casing, during the experiment.

As shown in Fig. 14 (a), when the impactor came in contact with the jelly roll, the cell experiences a significant voltage drop before reaching peak force, followed by a gradual decrease of voltage. Note that the voltage decline becomes steeper when the entire surface of the impactor traverses the cell's layer components. The jelly roll may undergo some local short circuits, and as the voltage subsequently recovers to a higher level, it suggests that the contact between the anode and cathode layers is no longer present. After reaching a minimum of 0.8 V, the voltage recovers to around 1.9 V. As can be observed in Fig. 14 (a), the recorded force at the minimum voltage is approximately 0.73 kN, while the simulated force at this point is nearly 0.7 kN. The measured temperature field for the jelly roll indicates that the highest temperatures are located near the battery terminals and at the fractured area in the middle when comparing Fig. 14(b) and (c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flat impact test: case study of 18,650 cell at drop test of height = 1 m and height = 2 m
ce:section.ce:para: A horizontal impact test was performed using a flat impactor weighing 24.62 kg at heights of 1 m and 2 m, respectively. The bottom surface of the impactor is completely flat, lead to that failure could occur across all layers. The scenarios defined here are related to delamination of layers and investigated how they might affect the electrolyte leakage and battery voltage. Figs. 15 (a)-(c) correspond to drop height of 1 m; and Figs. 15 (d)-(f), pertain to height of 2 m. In the experiment conducted at a height of 2 m, the casing shell is mostly flat, which lead to removal of the cap. Conversely, for the scenario with a height of 1 m, the casing shell exhibits some curvature towards the end (first case scenario). The curvature at the end of the ruptured layers, seen in Fig. 15 (a), acts as a barrier, partially impeding electrolyte leakage in the 1 m case scenario. However, in the 2 m case (second case scenario), the layers become completely flat after the rupture of the shell casing, allowing electrolyte to leak from both the lateral side and the terminal side of the battery. This results in faster electrolyte leakage at H = 2 m compared to H = 1 m, Fig. 16 .

As shown in Figs. 15 (d)-(f), due to the momentum of the impactor, some layers, including the electrolyte, spread through the air. Unlike the 1 m case scenario shown in Figs. 15 (a)-(c), we do not observe the same layer separation. In both cases, the temperature rise is negligible compared to the three-point bending test scenarios, though longitudinal failure of the separator is evident.

In the simulated deformation field results, the overall displacement magnitude, specifically presented in Figs. 15(c) and 15(f), we also observed that the cap is partially displaced. This displacement causes significant electrolyte leakage, leading to an earlier voltage drop compared to the scenario with a drop test conducted at height of 1 m, as shown in Fig. 16.

As illustrated in Fig. 17 , the battery cell encountered peak forces of approximately 51 kN for the drop heights of 2 m. After 1.2 ms, the voltage drops to nearly 0 for a period of 2.5 ms. Additionally, the experiments for both H = 1 and H = 2 m reveal that more regions in the separator experience failure in comparison with the three-point bending impact test.

The onset of the sudden voltage drop at a height of 1 m, as seen in Fig. 17, was observed at 0.1028 ms. Additionally, the detachment of the end cap, as shown in Fig. 15(e), began at 2.22 ms for the 2 m drop height, leading to its separation from the cylindrical shell. The simulation accurately captured the first peak following the voltage drop at 2.8 ms. The force–time trend for the H = 1 m compression test was similar to that of the H = 2 m case. However, due to limited repeatability—only one successful experiment—the results for the H = 1 m scenario are not presented.

Regarding the temperature field, due to the complete failure of the layers, the temperature rise was negligible. Consequently, we did not include the temperature distribution of the battery following the impact test.
Design recommendations and limitations for real-world application

The findings of this study provide insights into the mechanical response and potential failure modes of lithium-ion cells under impact conditions, based on numerical modelling and experimental impact tests. While the scope is limited to impact-induced failure, the proposed framework can support design considerations aimed at enhancing battery safety under mechanical abuse.
Based on the modelling methodology, several design rules can be recommended to minimise impact failures:
(1) strengthen the shell casing to better withstand mechanical loads;
(2) optimise the electrode and separator stacking to delay internal short-circuit initiation;
(3) integrate impact-detection sensors on the casing to provide early warning signs before critical failure occurs.

To reduce the risk of thermal runaway, battery packs can also incorporate reinforced casings, and the surrounding compartment can be optimised to absorb impact energy and minimise mechanical transmission to the cell structure. Improved separator materials and mechanical decoupling of internal components may further mitigate the initiation of critical failure pathways.

The present modelling approach does not account for long-term degradation, chemical ageing, or slow-evolving internal faults that may contribute to delayed thermal runaway. Therefore, the insights derived from this study should be used in conjunction with broader diagnostic tools and monitoring systems for real-world application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: This study provides a comprehensive investigation of the mechanical and thermal behaviour of 18,650 lithium-ion cells subjected to three-point bending and flat compression impact tests, as well as 26,650 cells under three-point bending impact tests. The experiments focused on three-point bending and horizontal impact tests, examining the effects of varying initial voltages at 15 % state of charge (SOC), different drop heights, and electrolyte leakage on temperature distribution and cell performance.

Different drop heights for three point bending tests were conducted, showing that the higher drops (3 m) caused more significant electrolyte leakage, and a notable voltage drop in the 18,650 cells. For the 26,550 cells, when the shell casing did not experience rupture, the temperature increase was more pronounced than any other case scenario, Height = 1 m and Height = 3 m. This study suggests that mechanical failure of the separator alone cannot fully account for thermal runaway; the rupture of the shell casing can be another critical factor to be considered. Thermal runaway during three point bending test might happen when there is no rupture in shell casing, but we see voltage drop or separator failure in internal layers of the battery. Temperature predictions were made for six different case scenario jelly roll and battery categories, considering variations in diameter and drop test heights ranging from 1 to 3 m.

In terms of the compression drop tests, it is revealed that the worst-case temperature scenario is not necessarily associated with greater drop heights. Furthermore, it is emphasised that voltage drops do not always correlate with temperature increases, especially in cases where cell failure occurs.

The innovative algorithm proposed here provides a means to modelling the amalgamation of multiple cells into a module and battery pack model, facilitating the study of coupled multi-physics responses to impact and crash loadings. Such capabilities could potentially empower electric vehicle manufacturers to better design protective structures around EV battery packs more efficiently and optimally.
